instruction: Demonstrate modern C++ lambda patterns including generic lambdas, mutable lambdas, immediately-invoked lambdas, recursive lambdas, and lambda captures with init-capture. Show how they replace std::bind and function objects.
output:
<think>
C++ lambdas have evolved significantly since C++11. Modern C++14/17/20 lambdas are far more powerful than std::bind and often replace custom function objects entirely.

Key patterns to show:
1. Generic lambdas (C++14): `auto fn = [](auto x) { return x * 2; }` — works like a template
2. Mutable lambdas: by default, captured-by-value variables are const. `mutable` allows modification
3. Init-capture (C++14): `[x = std::move(ptr)]` — move into capture, create new variables
4. Immediately-invoked lambda (IIFE): `const auto val = [&]{ complex_init(); return result; }();` — great for const initialization
5. Recursive lambdas: tricky because lambdas can't reference themselves. Solutions: std::function wrapper, or C++23 deducing-this
6. Lambda in constexpr context (C++17): lambdas can be constexpr
7. Templated lambdas (C++20): `[](std::integral auto x)` with concepts

I should contrast with std::bind: lambdas are almost always better. std::bind has issues with overloaded functions, is harder to read, and the compiler can't optimize it as well.

For the demo, I'll build practical examples: a sort comparator factory, a scope guard, a retry wrapper, and a visitor pattern — all using different lambda features.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <functional>
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <numeric>

// 1. Generic lambdas (C++14) — auto parameters
void demo_generic_lambda() {
    auto print_pair = [](const auto& a, const auto& b) {
        std::cout << "(" << a << ", " << b << ")\n";
    };

    print_pair(42, "hello");       // (int, const char*)
    print_pair(3.14, std::string("world")); // (double, string)

    // Generic comparator factory
    auto make_comparator = [](auto member_ptr) {
        return [member_ptr](const auto& a, const auto& b) {
            return a.*member_ptr < b.*member_ptr;
        };
    };

    struct Employee { std::string name; int salary; };
    std::vector<Employee> team = {{"Alice", 90000}, {"Bob", 75000}, {"Charlie", 100000}};
    std::sort(team.begin(), team.end(), make_comparator(&Employee::salary));
    std::cout << "Lowest salary: " << team.front().name << "\n";
}

// 2. Init-capture + move semantics (C++14)
void demo_init_capture() {
    auto ptr = std::make_unique<std::vector<int>>(std::vector<int>{1, 2, 3, 4, 5});

    // Move unique_ptr into lambda — can't copy, must move
    auto process = [data = std::move(ptr)]() {
        int sum = std::accumulate(data->begin(), data->end(), 0);
        std::cout << "Sum: " << sum << " (size: " << data->size() << ")\n";
    };
    // ptr is now nullptr
    process();

    // Init-capture with computation
    int base = 10;
    auto fn = [offset = base * 2 + 1](int x) { return x + offset; };
    std::cout << "fn(5) = " << fn(5) << "\n"; // 5 + 21 = 26
}

// 3. Mutable lambda — modify captured-by-value variables
void demo_mutable() {
    auto counter = [n = 0]() mutable -> int {
        return ++n;
    };
    std::cout << "Counter: " << counter() << ", " << counter() << ", " << counter() << "\n";
}

// 4. Immediately-invoked lambda for const initialization
void demo_iife() {
    const int mode = 2;

    // Complex initialization for a const variable
    const auto config = [&]() -> std::string {
        switch (mode) {
            case 1: return "debug";
            case 2: return "release";
            case 3: return "profile";
            default: return "unknown";
        }
    }(); // note: immediately invoked

    std::cout << "Config: " << config << "\n";
}

// 5. Recursive lambda via std::function
void demo_recursive() {
    // std::function wrapper enables self-reference
    std::function<int(int)> factorial = [&factorial](int n) -> int {
        return n <= 1 ? 1 : n * factorial(n - 1);
    };
    std::cout << "10! = " << factorial(10) << "\n";

    // Alternative: pass self as parameter (no std::function overhead)
    auto fib = [](auto self, int n) -> int {
        if (n <= 1) return n;
        return self(self, n - 1) + self(self, n - 2);
    };
    std::cout << "fib(10) = " << fib(fib, 10) << "\n";
}

// 6. Scope guard using lambda
template<typename F>
class ScopeGuard {
    F cleanup_;
    bool active_;
public:
    explicit ScopeGuard(F fn) : cleanup_(std::move(fn)), active_(true) {}
    ~ScopeGuard() { if (active_) cleanup_(); }
    void dismiss() { active_ = false; }
    ScopeGuard(ScopeGuard&& other) noexcept
        : cleanup_(std::move(other.cleanup_)), active_(other.active_) {
        other.active_ = false;
    }
};

template<typename F>
ScopeGuard<F> make_scope_guard(F fn) { return ScopeGuard<F>(std::move(fn)); }

void demo_scope_guard() {
    std::cout << "Entering scope\n";
    auto guard = make_scope_guard([]{
        std::cout << "Scope guard cleanup executed\n";
    });
    std::cout << "Working...\n";
    // guard's destructor runs cleanup automatically
}

int main() {
    std::cout << "=== Generic Lambdas ===\n";
    demo_generic_lambda();

    std::cout << "\n=== Init-Capture ===\n";
    demo_init_capture();

    std::cout << "\n=== Mutable Lambda ===\n";
    demo_mutable();

    std::cout << "\n=== IIFE ===\n";
    demo_iife();

    std::cout << "\n=== Recursive Lambda ===\n";
    demo_recursive();

    std::cout << "\n=== Scope Guard ===\n";
    demo_scope_guard();

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Build a std::function-based command pattern in C++ that supports undo/redo, command composition, and macro recording. Show how lambdas replace traditional command classes and how fold expressions enable variadic command composition.
output:
<think>
The command pattern traditionally requires a class hierarchy: Command base class with execute/undo virtual methods, concrete command classes for each operation. In modern C++, lambdas + std::function replace this entire hierarchy.

A command is just a pair of functions: execute and undo. `struct Command { std::function<void()> execute; std::function<void()> undo; }`. No inheritance needed.

For the command history (undo/redo):
- Maintain two stacks: undo_stack and redo_stack
- Execute: push to undo_stack, clear redo_stack
- Undo: pop from undo_stack, call undo(), push to redo_stack
- Redo: pop from redo_stack, call execute(), push to undo_stack

Composition: a composite command executes multiple commands in sequence. Undo reverses the sequence. With fold expressions (C++17): `(cmds.execute(), ...)` executes all commands.

Macro recording: start recording, execute commands normally (they're both executed and recorded), stop recording to produce a composite command that replays all recorded commands.

The lambda advantage: creating a command is one line, not a whole class. Captures hold the state needed for undo. For example, a "set value" command captures the old value in a lambda for undo.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <functional>
#include <vector>
#include <stack>
#include <string>
#include <numeric>

// --- Command: just a pair of functions ---

struct Command {
    std::string name;
    std::function<void()> execute;
    std::function<void()> undo;
};

// --- Command history with undo/redo ---

class CommandHistory {
    std::vector<Command> undo_stack_;
    std::vector<Command> redo_stack_;
    bool recording_ = false;
    std::vector<Command> macro_buffer_;

public:
    void execute(Command cmd) {
        cmd.execute();
        undo_stack_.push_back(std::move(cmd));
        redo_stack_.clear();
        if (recording_) {
            macro_buffer_.push_back(undo_stack_.back());
        }
    }

    bool undo() {
        if (undo_stack_.empty()) return false;
        auto cmd = std::move(undo_stack_.back());
        undo_stack_.pop_back();
        cmd.undo();
        redo_stack_.push_back(std::move(cmd));
        return true;
    }

    bool redo() {
        if (redo_stack_.empty()) return false;
        auto cmd = std::move(redo_stack_.back());
        redo_stack_.pop_back();
        cmd.execute();
        undo_stack_.push_back(std::move(cmd));
        return true;
    }

    void start_recording() {
        recording_ = true;
        macro_buffer_.clear();
    }

    Command stop_recording(const std::string& name) {
        recording_ = false;
        auto recorded = std::move(macro_buffer_);
        return compose(name, std::move(recorded));
    }

    // Compose multiple commands into one
    static Command compose(const std::string& name, std::vector<Command> cmds) {
        auto shared = std::make_shared<std::vector<Command>>(std::move(cmds));
        return Command{
            name,
            [shared]() {
                for (auto& cmd : *shared) cmd.execute();
            },
            [shared]() {
                for (auto it = shared->rbegin(); it != shared->rend(); ++it) {
                    it->undo();
                }
            }
        };
    }

    size_t undo_count() const { return undo_stack_.size(); }
    size_t redo_count() const { return redo_stack_.size(); }
};

// --- Command factories (lambdas replace command classes) ---

Command make_set_command(int& target, int new_value) {
    int old_value = target;
    return Command{
        "Set " + std::to_string(new_value),
        [&target, new_value]() { target = new_value; },
        [&target, old_value]() { target = old_value; }
    };
}

Command make_append_command(std::string& target, const std::string& text) {
    size_t old_len = target.size();
    return Command{
        "Append '" + text + "'",
        [&target, text]() { target += text; },
        [&target, old_len]() { target.resize(old_len); }
    };
}

Command make_push_back_command(std::vector<int>& vec, int value) {
    return Command{
        "Push " + std::to_string(value),
        [&vec, value]() { vec.push_back(value); },
        [&vec]() { vec.pop_back(); }
    };
}

// --- Variadic composition with fold expressions (C++17) ---

template<typename... Cmds>
Command compose_commands(const std::string& name, Cmds... cmds) {
    auto all = std::make_shared<std::vector<Command>>();
    (all->push_back(std::move(cmds)), ...); // fold expression
    return CommandHistory::compose(name, std::move(*all));
}

int main() {
    CommandHistory history;
    int counter = 0;
    std::string text;
    std::vector<int> numbers;

    // Execute individual commands
    history.execute(make_set_command(counter, 10));
    history.execute(make_append_command(text, "Hello"));
    history.execute(make_push_back_command(numbers, 42));

    std::cout << "After execute: counter=" << counter
              << " text='" << text << "' numbers.size=" << numbers.size() << "\n";

    // Undo
    history.undo();
    history.undo();
    std::cout << "After 2 undos: counter=" << counter
              << " text='" << text << "' numbers.size=" << numbers.size() << "\n";

    // Redo
    history.redo();
    std::cout << "After 1 redo: text='" << text << "'\n";

    // Composed command (variadic fold)
    auto batch = compose_commands("batch-update",
        make_set_command(counter, 99),
        make_append_command(text, " World"),
        make_push_back_command(numbers, 7)
    );
    history.execute(std::move(batch));
    std::cout << "After batch: counter=" << counter
              << " text='" << text << "' numbers=" << numbers.size() << "\n";

    // Single undo reverses entire batch
    history.undo();
    std::cout << "After batch undo: counter=" << counter
              << " text='" << text << "'\n";

    // Macro recording
    history.redo();
    history.start_recording();
    history.execute(make_set_command(counter, 200));
    history.execute(make_append_command(text, "!!!"));
    auto macro = history.stop_recording("my-macro");

    // Replay macro
    std::cout << "Before macro replay: counter=" << counter << " text='" << text << "'\n";
    history.execute(std::move(macro));
    std::cout << "After macro replay: counter=" << counter << " text='" << text << "'\n";

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement a compile-time computation system in C++ using constexpr functions, fold expressions, and template metaprogramming. Show how modern C++ enables functional-style computations that execute entirely at compile time.
output:
<think>
Modern C++ (17/20) allows significant computation at compile time through constexpr functions, consteval, fold expressions, and template metaprogramming. This enables type-safe, zero-runtime-cost computations.

I'll demonstrate:
1. constexpr functions: compile-time Fibonacci, factorial, string processing
2. Fold expressions (C++17): variadic template operations
3. constexpr std::array: compile-time data structures
4. static_assert: compile-time testing
5. consteval (C++20): guaranteed compile-time execution
6. Template metaprogramming for type-level computation

The practical applications: compile-time hash functions (for string switch), compile-time lookup tables, type-level state machines, and constexpr parsers.

Fold expressions are particularly interesting: `(args + ...)` expands to `a1 + a2 + a3 + ...`. Combined with constexpr, this enables compile-time reduction over parameter packs.

I'll build a compile-time string hash (FNV-1a) that enables switch statements on strings — something C++ can't do natively. Also a compile-time lookup table for sine values.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <array>
#include <cstdint>
#include <cmath>
#include <string_view>

// --- 1. Basic constexpr computation ---

constexpr int factorial(int n) {
    int result = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; ++i) result *= i;
    return result;
}

constexpr int fibonacci(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    int a = 0, b = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; ++i) {
        int c = a + b;
        a = b;
        b = c;
    }
    return b;
}

// Compile-time verification
static_assert(factorial(10) == 3628800);
static_assert(fibonacci(10) == 55);

// --- 2. Fold expressions for variadic operations ---

template<typename... Args>
constexpr auto sum(Args... args) {
    return (args + ...);  // unary right fold
}

template<typename... Args>
constexpr auto product(Args... args) {
    return (args * ...);
}

template<typename... Args>
constexpr auto min_of(Args... args) {
    auto result = (args, ...); // comma fold to get last, won't work
    // Use fold with lambda-like pattern
    auto m = std::common_type_t<Args...>{};
    bool first = true;
    ((first ? (m = args, first = false, 0) : (m = args < m ? args : m, 0)), ...);
    return m;
}

template<typename Pred, typename... Args>
constexpr int count_if(Pred pred, Args... args) {
    return (0 + ... + (pred(args) ? 1 : 0));
}

static_assert(sum(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) == 15);
static_assert(product(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) == 120);
static_assert(count_if([](int x) { return x > 3; }, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) == 2);

// --- 3. Compile-time string hash (FNV-1a) ---

constexpr uint64_t fnv1a_hash(std::string_view str) {
    uint64_t hash = 14695981039346656037ULL;
    for (char c : str) {
        hash ^= static_cast<uint64_t>(c);
        hash *= 1099511628211ULL;
    }
    return hash;
}

constexpr uint64_t operator""_hash(const char* str, size_t len) {
    return fnv1a_hash(std::string_view(str, len));
}

// Now we can "switch" on strings!
void process_command(std::string_view cmd) {
    switch (fnv1a_hash(cmd)) {
        case "start"_hash:  std::cout << "Starting...\n"; break;
        case "stop"_hash:   std::cout << "Stopping...\n"; break;
        case "status"_hash: std::cout << "Running.\n"; break;
        default:            std::cout << "Unknown: " << cmd << "\n";
    }
}

// --- 4. Compile-time lookup table ---

template<size_t N>
constexpr auto make_sin_table() {
    std::array<double, N> table{};
    for (size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        double angle = (2.0 * 3.14159265358979323846 * i) / N;
        // Taylor series for sin (constexpr-compatible)
        double x = angle;
        double term = x;
        double sum = term;
        for (int k = 1; k < 15; ++k) {
            term *= -x * x / ((2 * k) * (2 * k + 1));
            sum += term;
        }
        table[i] = sum;
    }
    return table;
}

constexpr auto SIN_TABLE = make_sin_table<256>();

// --- 5. Compile-time type-level computation ---

template<int... Values>
struct IntList {
    static constexpr size_t size = sizeof...(Values);
    static constexpr int sum = (Values + ... + 0);
    static constexpr auto to_array() {
        return std::array<int, sizeof...(Values)>{Values...};
    }
};

template<typename List, int V>
struct Append;

template<int... Vs, int V>
struct Append<IntList<Vs...>, V> {
    using type = IntList<Vs..., V>;
};

template<typename List, template<int> class Pred>
struct Filter;

template<template<int> class Pred>
struct Filter<IntList<>, Pred> {
    using type = IntList<>;
};

template<int Head, int... Tail, template<int> class Pred>
struct Filter<IntList<Head, Tail...>, Pred> {
    using Rest = typename Filter<IntList<Tail...>, Pred>::type;
    using type = std::conditional_t<
        Pred<Head>::value,
        typename Append<Rest, Head>::type,
        Rest
    >;
};

template<int V> struct IsEven { static constexpr bool value = (V % 2 == 0); };

using Numbers = IntList<1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8>;
using Evens = Filter<Numbers, IsEven>::type;

static_assert(Numbers::sum == 36);
static_assert(Evens::sum == 20);  // 2+4+6+8

int main() {
    // All these are compile-time constants
    constexpr auto f10 = factorial(10);
    constexpr auto fib20 = fibonacci(20);
    std::cout << "10! = " << f10 << "\n";
    std::cout << "fib(20) = " << fib20 << "\n";

    // String switch via compile-time hash
    process_command("start");
    process_command("status");
    process_command("unknown");

    // Compile-time sin table (zero runtime cost)
    std::cout << "sin(0) = " << SIN_TABLE[0] << "\n";
    std::cout << "sin(pi/2) ~= " << SIN_TABLE[64] << "\n";

    // Type-level computation results
    constexpr auto evens = Evens::to_array();
    std::cout << "Even numbers: ";
    for (int v : evens) std::cout << v << " ";
    std::cout << "\n";

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Show how to use std::bind alternatives in modern C++ — specifically how lambdas, partial application via lambdas, and std::invoke replace std::bind for cleaner, faster, and more readable code. Include CRTP and variadic forwarding patterns.
output:
<think>
std::bind was useful in C++11 but is largely superseded by lambdas in C++14+. The problems with std::bind:
1. Opaque return type — hard to debug
2. Doesn't work well with overloaded functions
3. Placeholder syntax (_1, _2) is confusing
4. Can't be optimized as well as lambdas (lambdas are unique types, bind returns a complex template)

Modern alternatives:
- Lambda with captures for partial application: `[&obj, val](auto&&... args) { return obj.method(val, std::forward<decltype(args)>(args)...); }`
- std::invoke (C++17): unified call syntax for functions, member functions, member pointers
- Perfect forwarding lambdas: `[](auto&&... args) -> decltype(auto) { return fn(std::forward<decltype(args)>(args)...); }`

I'll show:
1. std::bind vs lambda equivalents (side by side)
2. Partial application patterns
3. std::invoke for uniform callable invocation
4. CRTP for static polymorphism with callables
5. Variadic forwarding lambda patterns
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <functional>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <tuple>

// --- 1. std::bind vs lambda (side by side) ---

int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

void demo_bind_vs_lambda() {
    // OLD: std::bind
    auto add5_bind = std::bind(add, 5, std::placeholders::_1);
    // NEW: lambda (clearer, faster)
    auto add5_lambda = [](int x) { return add(5, x); };

    std::cout << "bind: " << add5_bind(3) << ", lambda: " << add5_lambda(3) << "\n";

    // bind FAILS with overloaded functions:
    // auto bound = std::bind(overloaded_fn, _1); // ambiguous!
    // Lambda works: auto fn = [](int x) { return overloaded_fn(x); };
}

// --- 2. Partial application via lambda ---

template<typename F, typename... BoundArgs>
auto partial(F&& f, BoundArgs&&... bound) {
    return [f = std::forward<F>(f),
            ...bound_args = std::forward<BoundArgs>(bound)]
           (auto&&... free_args) -> decltype(auto) {
        return f(bound_args..., std::forward<decltype(free_args)>(free_args)...);
    };
}

std::string format(const std::string& prefix, const std::string& sep,
                   const std::string& value) {
    return prefix + sep + value;
}

void demo_partial() {
    auto log_error = partial(format, std::string("[ERROR]"), std::string(": "));
    auto log_info  = partial(format, std::string("[INFO]"), std::string(": "));

    std::cout << log_error("disk full") << "\n";
    std::cout << log_info("server started") << "\n";
}

// --- 3. std::invoke — uniform callable syntax ---

struct Widget {
    int value = 42;
    int multiply(int factor) const { return value * factor; }
};

void demo_invoke() {
    Widget w{10};

    // std::invoke works with everything:
    auto r1 = std::invoke(add, 3, 4);                    // free function
    auto r2 = std::invoke(&Widget::multiply, w, 5);       // member function
    auto r3 = std::invoke(&Widget::value, w);              // member variable
    auto r4 = std::invoke([](int x) { return x * 2; }, 7); // lambda

    std::cout << "invoke results: " << r1 << ", " << r2
              << ", " << r3 << ", " << r4 << "\n";
}

// --- 4. CRTP for static polymorphism with callables ---

template<typename Derived>
class Callable {
public:
    template<typename... Args>
    decltype(auto) operator()(Args&&... args) {
        // Before hook
        auto& self = static_cast<Derived&>(*this);
        return self.call_impl(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
    }
};

class Logger : public Callable<Logger> {
public:
    void call_impl(const std::string& msg) {
        std::cout << "[LOG] " << msg << "\n";
    }
};

class Multiplier : public Callable<Multiplier> {
    int factor_;
public:
    explicit Multiplier(int f) : factor_(f) {}
    int call_impl(int x) { return x * factor_; }
};

// --- 5. Perfect-forwarding wrapper ---

template<typename F>
auto make_logged(F&& f) {
    return [fn = std::forward<F>(f)](auto&&... args) -> decltype(auto) {
        std::cout << "  calling with " << sizeof...(args) << " args\n";
        return fn(std::forward<decltype(args)>(args)...);
    };
}

// --- 6. Apply tuple as function arguments ---

template<typename F, typename Tuple>
decltype(auto) apply_tuple(F&& f, Tuple&& t) {
    return std::apply(std::forward<F>(f), std::forward<Tuple>(t));
}

int main() {
    std::cout << "=== Bind vs Lambda ===\n";
    demo_bind_vs_lambda();

    std::cout << "\n=== Partial Application ===\n";
    demo_partial();

    std::cout << "\n=== std::invoke ===\n";
    demo_invoke();

    std::cout << "\n=== CRTP Callables ===\n";
    Logger logger;
    logger("hello from CRTP");
    Multiplier mul3(3);
    std::cout << "3 * 7 = " << mul3(7) << "\n";

    std::cout << "\n=== Perfect-Forwarding Wrapper ===\n";
    auto logged_add = make_logged(add);
    std::cout << "Result: " << logged_add(3, 4) << "\n";

    std::cout << "\n=== Apply Tuple ===\n";
    auto args = std::make_tuple(10, 20);
    std::cout << "apply_tuple(add, (10,20)) = " << apply_tuple(add, args) << "\n";

    return 0;
}
```
